FT_IC1.evidence: Data collected from adolescent and parent/caregiver dyads enrolled in the ABCD Study from baseline through 3-year follow-up. ABCD Study is an ongoing prospective cohort study of ~11,800 adolescents (ages 9-10 at baseline) recruited from over 21 study sites across the US.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using mixed-effects modeling on longitudinal data with self-report data collected from baseline through 3-year follow-up. Reports regression coefficient: β = 1.5, p < 0.001.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Issues in Mental Health Nursing, 2025.

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic social media use (PSMU) operationalized via Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), a problematic/addictive SM scale. PSMU examined as IV/outcome paired with ACEs exposure and emotional/behavioral dysregulation (individual-trait variables).

FT_IC3.evidence: Significant positive association between cumulative ACEs score at 10-12 years and higher PSMU at 12-14 years: β = 1.5, p < 0.001.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric result reported: regression coefficient β = 1.5 (p < 0.001) for ACEs-PSMU association.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional and longitudinal design with PSMU as measured outcome across waves; not DV-only design.
notes: PSMU is examined using SMAQ scale, which qualifies as problematic SM operationalization.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study included 8324 children from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, followed annually for 4 years (ages 9 to 10 years at baseline from 21 data collection sites across the United States).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using linear mixed models to examine associations between different types of digital media use and ADHD-related symptoms assessed via parent-reported Child Behavior Checklist.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics Open Science, 2026.

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use defined as time on social networking sites (Facebook, Instagram) and video chat (Skype, FaceTime). Measured as IV in longitudinal analysis and paired with ADHD-related symptoms (inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity), both individual-trait behavioral outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Average social media use associated with increased inattention symptoms over time (social media × time: β = 0.03, p < 0.001), with cumulative 4-year effect β = 0.15.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results: longitudinal interaction coefficient β = 0.03 (p < 0.001) for social media × time on inattention; cumulative effect β = 0.15 (SE 0.03, p < 0.001).

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design with social media as IV predicting ADHD outcomes. Reverse causality tested; inattention symptoms did not predict social media use (β = -0.002, p = 0.64). EC7 does not apply.
notes: Study reports unidirectional association: social media use predicts inattention, not vice versa.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, including 1940 participants aged 11 to 12 years at Wave 1, analyzed over three annual waves (Waves 1-3 from ABCD 5.1 data release).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis investigating longitudinal relationships between social media usage and binge eating using Modified Poisson regression with cross-sectional and prospective associations.

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media addiction (SMA) scores measured via six-item questionnaire adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale, paired with binge eating symptoms (individual-trait behavioral outcome). Time spent on social media also measured but primarily SMA drove associations.

FT_IC3.evidence: SMA scores were prospectively associated with binge eating. Addiction SMA group showed increased risk of BE at Wave 3 (RR 1.90, 95% CI: 1.10-3.27).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results: Cross-sectional PR 2.17 (95% CI: 1.22-3.84) at Wave 1; prospective RR 1.90 (95% CI: 1.10-3.27) for addiction SMA; trajectory analysis RR 3.01 (95% CI: 2.07-4.37) for consistently high SMA.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional and longitudinal design with social media addiction as IV predicting binge eating outcomes. EC7 does not apply.
notes: SMA (problematic/addictive SM) showed stronger prospective associations with BE than time spent on social media alone.
